narrative:
Dear Student,

I understand that receiving a rejection for your university application can be disheartening, and I want to help you understand the reasons behind this decision. The model used to evaluate your application predicted a 78.7% probability that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt. This prediction led to the rejection of your application, as the university aims to admit students who are likely to succeed in passing the bar exam.

Let's look at the factors that influenced this prediction. Your academic performance, particularly your grades in Year 1 and Year 3, were significant contributors. Your decile rankings in these years were lower than the average, which suggested to the model that your academic performance might not be as strong as other applicants. Additionally, your LSAT score, which was below the average, also played a crucial role in the prediction. These academic indicators were the most influential in pushing the prediction towards a potential failure.

Your family income, categorized as middle, and your racial background as Asian were also considered, but they had a lesser impact compared to your academic performance. Your undergraduate GPA was slightly below average, which further contributed to the prediction of not passing. Other factors, such as your full-time status and gender, had minimal influence on the decision.

Given this information, you might consider focusing on improving your academic performance and LSAT score if you plan to reapply in the future. Enhancing these areas could significantly increase your chances of passing the bar exam and, consequently, strengthen your application. Remember, this prediction is not a reflection of your potential but rather an assessment based on current data. With dedication and effort, you can improve your prospects and achieve your goals.
